title: A Serious Call to Accountability
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 9-22-24 -PM- A Serious Call to Accountability - Hebrews 3_12-15 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: A Serious Warning Given to Believers
subpoints: The warning in Hebrews 3:12-15 is addressed to brethren — those who profess Christ
The consequence of not heeding the warning is a hardened heart
A hardened heart is one that has stopped hearing the voice of God and acts according to its own desires

outline.title: The Progressive Steps Toward a Hardened Heart
subpoints: Step 1: Giving way to sin and allowing evil into our lives
Step 2: When conscience is activated, the choice is either to repent or to justify the sin
Step 3: Self-deception — calling good what God calls evil builds a system of self-atonement
Step 4: Unbelief — failing to believe God's accurate diagnosis of our sin
Step 5: Departing — drifting away from God until the anchor rope of faith is cut
Step 6: The hardened heart — what once bothered us no longer does

outline.title: Why We Need Others: The Blinding Power of Sin
subpoints: Sin deceives 10 out of 10 people — no one is exempt
Spiritual blindness differs from physical blindness: the spiritually blind person does not know they are blind
We all have blind spots where we cannot see our own sin
Personal spiritual insight is the product of community

outline.title: The Essential Call to Exhort One Another Daily
subpoints: Hebrews 3:13 commands daily mutual exhortation — not weekly or occasionally
The category of individualistic, independent Christianity does not exist in Scripture
It is our responsibility to place ourselves in accountability — not to wait for others to seek us out
The example of Nathan and David: the Word of God held up to David through another person
Christians who live outside God's system of help are in danger of increasing blindness and a hardened heart

summary: In this Sunday evening sermon, Pastor Tito Lyro expounds Hebrews 3:12-15, delivering a serious call to accountability in the body of Christ. He shows that the warning is addressed to believers — those called "brethren" and "partakers of Christ" — and traces the progressive steps by which an undetected, unexposed, and unforsaken sin can lead a Christian toward a hardened heart. The progression moves from giving way to sin, to self-deception and justification, to unbelief in God's diagnosis, to a gradual departure from God, and finally to a heart so hardened that it no longer responds to the Spirit. At the center of the sermon is the blinding power of sin: whereas the physically blind person knows they are blind, the spiritually blind person does not — which is why every Christian desperately needs others in their life. Pastor Lyro draws on the example of Nathan confronting David to illustrate the ministry of holding the Word of God before a blind brother or sister. He dismantles the excuse that people aren't seeking us out, insisting it is our responsibility to place ourselves in accountability. The sermon ends with a call for every believer to open their heart to the community of the church so that through mutual exhortation hearts are softened and shaped into the image of Christ.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading

For our scripture reading this afternoon, let us turn to the book of Hebrews chapter 3, reading verses 12 through 15.

*Beware brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief, in departing from the living God. But exhort one another daily, while it is called today, lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin. For we have become partakers of Christ, if we hold the beginning of our confidence steadfast to the end. While it is said today, if you will hear his voice, do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion.*

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, thank you for your word. We pray you minister to us this afternoon. We pray that you help us to stay attentive to the proclamation of your word. We pray that your Spirit would work in our hearts to love you more, to be changed, to be like Jesus Christ. In Jesus' name, Amen.

## Introduction: Accountability in the Christian Life

One of the things that we are to do by God's grace in resisting the works of the flesh — as we saw this morning — is to ask for help from a brother or sister who does not struggle with the same sin as we do. This idea of accountability is very important in the Christian life, and the Holy Spirit calls us to it. It is a serious call to accountability.

Our goal for this afternoon is to look at this passage — to see how it teaches that we are to hold each other accountable, and how this passage destroys the idea that you can be an independent, individualistic Christian. That category does not exist in the Scriptures.

One commentator said: *This passage puts before us a critical warning and an essential call that together reinforce the presence and power of remaining sin, and the need for the daily ministry of the body of Christ in the life of every member — pastor included.*

This warning is given to those who profess faith in Christ. In verse 12, the author refers to his audience as *brethren.* In verse 14, these people are called *partakers of Christ.* The Holy Spirit is not speaking to those outside of Christ, but to us — wearing the church — and the necessity of accountability is for everyone in the church of the Lord Jesus Christ.

## A Serious Warning

Verse 12 opens with a strong word: *Beware* (or *take care*, or *see to it*) — *lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief.* The seriousness of the warning is also seen in the consequence of not listening: if we don't listen to this warning, we will have a hardened heart. To have a hardened heart, according to Leon Morris, is to stop hearing the voice of God and to act according to one's own desires. If we don't have each other in our lives, that is going to happen.

## The Progressive Steps Toward a Hardened Heart

What we read here is a picture of what sin does if it is undetected, unexposed, or unforsaken.

**Step 1: Allowing sin into our lives.** We allow things into our lives that are outside the boundaries of what God has called us to be and do — things that God would name as evil. Because we are believers and the heart of stone has been replaced with a heart of flesh, our conscience bothers us when we sin and the Holy Spirit mercifully convicts us.

**Step 2: The choice.** When our conscience is activated, we face two options. The first and best choice is to admit that what we have done is wrong and place ourselves again on the mercies of Christ, receiving his forgiveness. Or we can erect a system of self-atonement — essentially arguing for the rightness of what we have done. Guilt is a mercy of God. To get rid of just the guilt is like taking aspirin for a bacterial infection — the pain may be gone, but what's causing it is still there.

**Step 3: Self-deception.** Every person living with sin is a skilled self-deceiver. We are very good at finding reasons why we don't have to obey God. A commentator illustrates: the pastor who became angry during an elders' meeting tells himself he was not angry — he was just speaking like one of God's prophets. The husband and wife gossiping about someone all the way home tell themselves it is not gossip, just a vague extended detailed prayer request. The tight-fisted businessman tells himself he has been a good steward of the resources God entrusted to him.

**Step 4: Unbelief.** Verse 12: *an evil heart of unbelief.* Rather than simple faith and rest in the accurate diagnosis of the Word of God and the sufficient grace of Christ, we argue to ourselves that what we have done is not in fact wrong. This is an act of pride, rebellion, and unbelief — because you are failing to believe what God says concerning your actions.

**Step 5: Departing.** Verse 12: *in departing from the living God.* This word is related to the word *apostasy* and means to fall away. What anchors us against the storms of temptation is a firm acceptance of what the Bible says concerning our sins and a firm rest in the grace of Christ. When we cut that anchor rope, we always end up drifting further.

**Step 6: The hardened heart.** Where we finally end is with a hardened heart. What once bothered us doesn't bother us anymore. What we knew was outside of God's boundaries now lives inside our boundaries, and it doesn't matter to us anymore. It is a reversal to that stony heart — no longer malleable, no longer shapeable, hard and resistant to change, no longer tender and responsive to the hands of the Holy Spirit.

## Why We Need Others: Spiritual Blindness

How can this happen in the life of a Christian? Because sin is fundamentally deceptive. Sin deceives 10 out of 10 people. There is nobody in this room who is not susceptible to being deceived by sin.

And spiritual blindness is not like physical blindness. The physically blind person knows they are physically blind — they do not go around claiming they can see. But the spiritually blind person does not know that they are spiritually blind. They are blind to their own blindness. The spiritually blind person walks around with the delusion that no one has a more accurate view of them than they do.

We all have spots in our lives where we can't see — sins in our lives that we can't see because sin is deceitful. That is why we need people in our lives who can see those blind spots, who can see the sin in our hearts that deceives us into thinking it is not there.

## The Essential Call: Exhort One Another Daily

Verse 13: *But exhort one another daily while it is called today, lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin.*

The call is to encourage each other *daily.* Why daily? Because the blinding ability of sin is so powerful and so persuasive that you and I literally need daily intervention. The writer of Hebrews is destroying with this warning any allegiance we might have to an isolated, individualized Jesus-and-me Christianity. That does not exist in the Scriptures. We are saved into a body — the church of the Lord Jesus Christ — and we need those people around us.

None of us is wired to live this Christian life alone. None of us is safe living separated and unknown from each other. Each of us — whether pastor or congregant — needs the eyes of others in order to see ourselves with clarity and accuracy. We hold the mirror of the Word of God to ourselves but also to others, that they might see what is going on in their own hearts.

The example: when we go through 2 Samuel 11 and 12, David commits his sin with Bathsheba, kills her husband to cover it — and then Nathan comes in chapter 12. What is Nathan doing? He is holding the Word of God before David in a creative way — with the story of the little lamb — so that David can see what he was not able to see apart from Nathan's work in his life. And that is what we are called to do in each other's lives.

## Personal Responsibility

The lawyers in our hearts may argue: *I would love this kind of accountability, but no one in the church seeks me out.* But the passage does not say that people need to seek you out. It is your responsibility to place yourself in accountability to other believers. It is your responsibility to seek people out and open your hearts to them. If you are making that argument, all you are saying is that you don't want to be accountable.

And perhaps the lawyer says: *I tried it once and people hurt me. Never again.* People will fail you — but that does not deny what the Word of God says: you need people in your life.

God in his infinite mercy and grace did not leave us to go through this life alone. He brought us into his church so that together we would grow in faith. Each one of us is a gift of grace to the others. To ignore our spiritual need for one another is to set out on the path that ends in a hardened heart toward our Lord Jesus Christ.

So let us exhort each other in the grace of Jesus Christ as long as it is today. Let us be accountable to one another. Let us open our hearts to each other and listen to what the brethren say — so that we may repent and grow to be more like Jesus Christ. And as we do that, our hearts are softened and shaped, and the Spirit shapes them in the form and image of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Father, in heaven, thank you that you are good. Thank you for your word that is powerful. We pray that we will be people who are accountable to one another. We pray that you help us to see our spiritual blindness. We pray that we will be a blessing to others in that area as well. In Jesus' name, Amen.
